title: Classes, fields and methods

canonicalExplanation.summary: A Java class combines private representation with constructors and cohesive methods that preserve invariants and expose the smallest useful public interface.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A Counter keeps its value private, rejects a negative starting value, and exposes increment and value methods without returning mutable internals. Your goal is to design and implement a class with private representation and cohesive methods. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Method contracts and parameters; pf data records. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: A Java class combines private representation with constructors and cohesive methods that preserve invariants and expose the smallest useful public interface. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Getters can leak mutable state, public fields bypass validation, static state is shared across instances, and encapsulation is about enforceable contracts rather than accessor count. The learner must reject this documented misconception: A class is encapsulated as long as every field has a public getter and setter.

includes: Encapsulate state and behavior behind a minimal public interface.
Design and implement a class with private representation and cohesive methods.
excludes: Getters can leak mutable state, public fields bypass validation, static state is shared across instances, and encapsulation is about enforceable contracts rather than accessor count.

outcomes: Design and implement a class with private representation and cohesive methods.

examples.title: Classes, fields and methods: direct language case
examples.situation: A Counter keeps its value private, rejects a negative starting value, and exposes increment and value methods without returning mutable internals.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A Counter keeps its value private, rejects a negative starting value, and exposes increment and value methods without returning mutable internals.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Keep representation private and expose behavior that preserves invariants; do not publish mutation merely to mirror each field.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Keep representation private and expose behavior that preserves invariants; do not publish mutation merely to mirror each field.

examples.title: Classes, fields and methods: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: A class returns List.copyOf for a snapshot instead of returning the owned mutable list directly to callers.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: A class returns List.copyOf for a snapshot instead of returning the owned mutable list directly to callers.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Getters can leak mutable state, public fields bypass validation, static state is shared across instances, and encapsulation is about enforceable contracts rather than accessor count.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: A class is encapsulated as long as every field has a public getter and setter.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Design and implement a class with private representation and cohesive methods.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A class is encapsulated as long as every field has a public getter and setter.
misconceptions.correction: Keep representation private and expose behavior that preserves invariants; do not publish mutation merely to mirror each field.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: A class is encapsulated as long as every field has a public getter and setter.

analogy.example: a secured service object whose constructor admits only valid state and whose public desk exposes approved behavior can introduce Classes, fields and methods, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: understanding encapsulation, construction, identity, equality, and records
limitations: object references, hashing, shallow immutability, and garbage collection are not physical ownership rules

recap.summary: You should now be able to design and implement a class with private representation and cohesive methods. Keep this boundary in view: Getters can leak mutable state, public fields bypass validation, static state is shared across instances, and encapsulation is about enforceable contracts rather than accessor count.
